reviews.summary: Consistent4D is a novel approach for generating 4D dynamic objects from uncalibrated monocular videos, leveraging a 3D-aware image diffusion model and a cascade DyNeRF architecture to achieve spatial and temporal consistency.
strengths: The paper presents a novel approach for 4D dynamic object generation from a single-view uncalibrated video.
The proposed method leverages a 3D-aware image diffusion model and a cascade DyNeRF architecture to achieve spatial and temporal consistency.
The paper provides extensive experiments and comparisons with other methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
The authors provide a clear and well-organized presentation of the method and its components.
weaknesses: The paper relies on a pre-trained diffusion model, which may limit the generalization ability of the method.
The performance of the model relies on the quality of the input video, and noisy input videos may lead to poor results.
The training of the model is computationally expensive and may present a challenge for practical deployment.
The paper does not provide a thorough analysis of the limitations and potential biases of the proposed method.
questions: How does the proposed method handle complex or abrupt motion in the input video?
Can the authors provide more details on the pre-trained diffusion model used in the method?
How does the method handle noisy or low-quality input videos?
Can the authors provide more information on the computational resources required for training the model?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge several limitations of the proposed method, including its reliance on a pre-trained diffusion model, its sensitivity to input video quality, and its computationally expensive training process.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel differential privacy mechanism for massive data analysis, integrating the Bag of Little Bootstraps (BLB) method with a Gaussian differential privacy mechanism. The authors provide a general, scalable differentially private algorithm adaptable to various methods and analyze asymptotic distribution of statistics using Nonlinear expectation theory.
strengths: The paper provides a comprehensive overview of the existing literature on differential privacy and its applications in massive data analysis.
The authors propose a novel differential privacy mechanism that integrates the BLB method with a Gaussian differential privacy mechanism.
The paper provides a detailed analysis of the asymptotic distribution of statistics using Nonlinear expectation theory.
The authors provide a general, scalable differentially private algorithm adaptable to various methods.
weaknesses: The paper assumes uniform privacy requirements across all bags, which may not be realistic in practice.
The authors do not provide a thorough comparison with existing differential privacy mechanisms.
The paper assumes that the true parameter value is known, which may not be the case in practice.
The authors do not provide a detailed discussion on the computational complexity of the proposed algorithm.

Can the authors provide a more detailed discussion on the assumptions made in the paper?

reviews.summary: This paper investigates the convergence rate of Deep Equilibrium Models (DEQs) with general activations, extending the previous work on ReLU activation. The authors propose a novel population Gram matrix and a new form of dual activation with Hermite polynomial expansion to bound the least eigenvalue of the Gram matrix. They show that the gradient descent algorithm converges to a global optimum at a linear rate for the over-parametrized DEQs with L-bounded activation functions.
strengths: The paper provides a generalization of the previous work on ReLU activation to general activations.
The authors propose a novel population Gram matrix and a new form of dual activation with Hermite polynomial expansion.
The paper shows that the gradient descent algorithm converges to a global optimum at a linear rate for the over-parametrized DEQs with L-bounded activation functions.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the activation function has bounded first and second derivatives, which may not be true for all activation functions.
The authors use a novel population Gram matrix and a new form of dual activation with Hermite polynomial expansion, which may be complex to implement.
The paper assumes that the learning rate is small enough, which may not be the case in practice.
questions: How do the results of this paper generalize to other types of activation functions?

reviews.limitations: The paper assumes that the activation function has bounded first and second derivatives, which may not be true for all activation functions.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes MBR and QE finetuning, which distill the quality gains from MBR decoding and QE reranking at training time, while using an efficient decoding algorithm at inference time. The authors show that these finetuning methods significantly outperform the base model on the task of Neural Machine Translation (NMT) and achieve additional quality improvements when using an external LLM as a teacher model.
strengths: The paper proposes a novel and effective approach to distill the quality gains from MBR decoding and QE reranking at training time.
The authors provide a thorough experimental evaluation of their approach on the task of NMT, including human evaluation and automatic metrics.
The paper explores the use of an external LLM as a teacher model, which leads to significant performance improvements.
The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of the effects of different decoding strategies on the performance of MBR-ﬁnetuned and QE-ﬁnetuned models.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the MBR and QE utility functions are available, which may not be the case in all scenarios.
The authors do not provide a thorough analysis of the computational cost of generating the MBR and QE data.
The paper does not explore the use of other decoding algorithms, such as top-k sampling or nucleus sampling.
The authors do not provide a clear explanation of why the QE-ﬁnetuned model outperforms the MBR-ﬁnetuned model in some cases.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes IDEA, an Invariant Causal Defense method against adversarial attacks on graph neural networks. IDEA learns causal features that have strong and invariant predictability across attacks, achieving state-of-the-art defense performance under both evasion and poisoning attacks on five benchmark datasets.
strengths: The paper proposes a novel invariant causal defense perspective, which is a promising new direction in the field of graph adversarial robustness.
The authors provide a comprehensive analysis of the causal relationships between nodes in graph adversarial attacks, which is a key contribution of the paper.
The proposed IDEA method is theoretically sound and has been experimentally validated on five benchmark datasets.
The paper provides a thorough comparison with state-of-the-art defense methods, demonstrating the superiority of IDEA in terms of robustness and accuracy.
weaknesses: The paper assumes a linear causal relationship between the causal feature and the label, which may not hold in all cases.
The authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed method.
The paper assumes that the attack domain is known, which may not be the case in practice.
The authors do not provide a clear explanation of how the domain learner is trained.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Synapse, a computer agent that uses a novel prompting technique to improve multi-step decision-making in computer control tasks. Synapse consists of three main components: state abstraction, trajectory-as-exemplar prompting, and exemplar memory. The authors evaluate Synapse on two benchmarks, MiniWoB++ and Mind2Web, and show that it outperforms existing methods in terms of success rate and data efficiency.

The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their method on two benchmarks, MiniWoB++ and Mind2Web.
The paper discusses the limitations of existing methods and how Synapse addresses these limitations.
The authors provide a clear and concise explanation of their method and its components.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the LLM can handle long HTML documents, which may not be the case in practice.
The authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the performance of Synapse on tasks with complex states.
The paper does not discuss the potential risks of using Synapse in real-world applications.
The authors do not provide a clear explanation of how the exemplar memory is constructed and retrieved.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a refined function-space variational inference method for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) that improves uncertainty estimation by incorporating an informative function space prior. The proposed method, called R-FVI, uses empirical Bayes to learn the parameters of the prior distribution from the training set and constructs the function space prior by sampling useful predictive functions via Bayesian model averaging (BMA). The method also introduces a categorical latent variable to allow sample functions to have varying degrees of disagreement depending on the status of the predictive input. The authors evaluate the proposed method on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms existing methods in terms of uncertainty estimation.
strengths: The paper proposes a novel and effective method for improving uncertainty estimation in BNNs.
The method is well-motivated and grounded in Bayesian model averaging (BMA).
The authors provide a clear and concise description of the proposed method and its implementation.
The experimental results are thorough and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
weaknesses: The paper assumes a specific architecture for the BNN, which may limit its applicability to other architectures.
The method requires careful tuning of hyperparameters, which can be time-consuming and may not be feasible for large-scale datasets.
The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed method.
The authors do not discuss potential limitations or challenges of the proposed method.
questions: How does the proposed method perform on more complex datasets or architectures?
Can the method be extended to other types of neural networks, such as recurrent neural networks?
How does the method handle cases where the training set is small or noisy?
Can the method be used for other applications, such as regression or reinforcement learning?
reviews.limitations: The paper does not adequately address the limitations of the proposed method, such as its reliance on careful hyperparameter tuning and its potential computational complexity.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel post-hoc zero-shot OOD detection method called NegLabel, which leverages a large set of negative labels to enhance the distinction between ID and OOD samples. The method uses a NegMining algorithm to select high-quality negative labels and a novel scheme for the OOD score that effectively combines the knowledge of VLMs.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new forward learning framework for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) called FORWARD GNN, which avoids the constraints imposed by backpropagation (BP) via an effective layer-wise local forward training. The authors extend the original forward-forward algorithm (FF) to deal with graph data and GNNs, and make it possible to operate without generating negative inputs. They also enable each layer to learn from both the bottom-up and top-down signals without relying on the backpropagation of errors. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show the effectiveness and generality of the proposed forward graph learning framework.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes VBPI-Mixtures, a novel algorithm for Bayesian phylogenetic inference that combines Variational Bayesian Phylogenetic Inference (VBPI) with mixture learning in black-box variational inference (BBVI). The authors demonstrate that mixtures of subsplit Bayesian nets (SBNs) can approximate distributions that a single SBN cannot, and experimentally show that VBPI-Mixtures achieves state-of-the-art results in terms of marginal log-likelihood estimation and produces more accurate approximations of the tree-topology posterior.
strengths: The paper proposes a novel and effective algorithm for Bayesian phylogenetic inference
The authors provide a clear and concise explanation of the method and its benefits

The paper provides a thorough review of the related work and clearly positions VBPI-Mixtures as a state-of-the-art method
weaknesses: The paper assumes a uniform prior over all unrooted trees, which may not be realistic in all cases
The authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the computational complexity of VBPI-Mixtures
The paper does not discuss the potential limitations of using mixture learning in BBVI
The experimental results are based on a limited number of datasets and may not generalize to other cases

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel hallucination detection method called INSIDE, which leverages the internal states of LLMs to detect hallucinations. The method consists of two components: EigenScore, a metric that measures the semantic consistency of responses in the embedding space, and feature clipping, a technique that truncates extreme activations in the internal states to reduce overconfident generations.
strengths: The paper presents a novel and effective approach to hallucination detection, which has not been explored before.
The EigenScore metric is a simple yet effective way to measure the semantic consistency of responses in the embedding space.
The feature clipping technique is a useful addition to the method, which can help reduce overconfident generations.
The paper provides extensive experiments and ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
The method is shown to outperform several state-of-the-art methods on several popular LLMs and QA benchmarks.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the internal states of LLMs retain the power of hallucination detection, which may not always be the case.
The method relies on the availability of multiple generations, which may not be feasible in all scenarios.
The feature clipping technique may not be effective in all cases, and its performance may depend on the specific LLM and task.
The paper does not provide a thorough analysis of the limitations and potential biases of the proposed method.
The method may not be applicable to black-box models, where users cannot access the internal states.

reviews.summary: This paper studies robust memorization with neural networks, which is the ability of a network to memorize a finite classification dataset while being robust to certain types of noise. The authors prove that it is NP-hard to compute robust memorization for certain simple networks, and provide necessary conditions for the existence of optimal robust networks. They also construct optimal robust memorization networks for a given dataset in polynomial time and for any binary classification dataset by controlling the Lipschitz constant of the network.

reviews.summary: This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the behavior of gradient descent (GD) in matrix factorization, particularly when using large initialization. The authors employ a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) argument and an inductive argument to analyze the GD trajectory from the initial point to the global minima, and show that even with large initialization, GD can exhibit incremental learning.
strengths: The paper provides a detailed and rigorous analysis of the behavior of GD in matrix factorization, particularly when using large initialization.
The authors employ a novel SNR argument and an inductive argument to analyze the GD trajectory, which is a significant contribution to the field.
The paper provides a clear and concise presentation of the results, with well-organized sections and accurate citations of prior work.
weaknesses: The paper assumes a strictly decreasing top eigenvalue, which may not be a realistic assumption in all cases.
The analysis focuses on the simplest matrix factorization setting, and it is unclear how the results would generalize to more complex settings.
The paper does not provide a clear explanation of the transition assumption, which is a critical component of the analysis.
questions: How do the results of this paper generalize to more complex settings, such as matrix sensing or deep matrix factorization?
Can the transition assumption be relaxed or removed, and if so, how would this affect the analysis?
What are the implications of this work for the design of initialization strategies for deep learning algorithms?
reviews.limitations: The paper acknowledges several limitations, including the assumption of a strictly decreasing top eigenvalue, the focus on the simplest matrix factorization setting, and the lack of a clear explanation of the transition assumption.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces LATM, a closed-loop framework that enables large language models to create and utilize their own tools for diverse tasks, improving performance and reducing computational costs.

weaknesses: The paper assumes that the tool maker can always generate a correct tool, which may not be the case in practice.
The authors do not provide a thorough analysis of the potential risks and limitations of their approach, such as the potential for suboptimal or incorrect tools.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a novel representation for binary programs, called DDG Fingerprinting, which captures semantic similarity and structural properties. The authors use data dependency graphs (DDGs) to represent programs and hash them using the Weisfeiler-Lehman graph hashing algorithm. They then construct a metric space using Hamming Codes and use k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) to search for similar programs. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach on a dataset of malicious and benign programs, showing that it can identify patterns in novel malware with functionality not previously identified.
strengths: The paper presents a novel and interesting approach to representing binary programs, which captures both semantic similarity and structural properties.

The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach on a dataset of malicious and benign programs, showing that it can identify patterns in novel malware with functionality not previously identified.
The paper provides a clear and concise description of the methodology and results, making it easy to follow and understand.
The authors discuss the limitations and potential applications of their approach, providing a comprehensive overview of the work.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the dataset of malicious and benign programs is representative of the broader population of binary programs, which may not be the case.
The authors do not provide a thorough evaluation of the performance of their approach on a diverse range of datasets, which limits the generalizability of the results.

The paper does not provide a clear explanation of how the Weisfeiler-Lehman graph hashing algorithm is used to hash the DDGs, which may be a limitation for readers who are not familiar with graph hashing techniques.
The authors do not discuss the potential security implications of their approach, which may be a concern for readers who are interested in the security applications of the work.
questions: How does the authors' approach compare to other existing approaches to representing binary programs?
What are the potential limitations of the Weisfeiler-Lehman graph hashing algorithm in this context?
How does the authors' approach handle cases where the DDGs are not isomorphic?
What are the potential applications of the authors' approach in the field of malware analysis?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge that their approach assumes a representative dataset of malicious and benign programs, which may not be the case in practice. They also note that the Weisfeiler-Lehman graph hashing algorithm may not be suitable for all types of DDGs.

reviews.summary: MuSR is a new dataset for evaluating language models on multistep soft reasoning tasks specified in a natural language narrative. The dataset is created through a novel neurosymbolic synthetic-to-natural generation algorithm, enabling the construction of complex reasoning instances that challenge GPT-4. The authors evaluate a range of LLMs and prompting techniques on this dataset and characterize the gaps that remain for techniques like chain-of-thought to perform robust reasoning.
strengths: The authors introduce a new reasoning benchmark, MuSR, consisting of 756 total examples across three domains that challenge state-of-the-art models.
The dataset is created through a novel neurosymbolic synthetic-to-natural generation algorithm, enabling the construction of complex reasoning instances that challenge GPT-4.
The authors evaluate a range of LLMs and prompting techniques on this dataset and characterize the gaps that remain for techniques like chain-of-thought to perform robust reasoning.
weaknesses: The authors acknowledge that the method can create reasoning trees of varying depths, but found that shallower trees provide the best level of detail for creating a narrative.
The authors experimented with validation on Amazon Mechanical Turk, but found that many workers performed very badly in qualification rounds.

The authors note that using weaker models to create examples approaching the quality of GPT-4 requires minor editing of prompts, introduction of more detailed self-refine prompts, and increase in temperature iteratively per retry.
questions: How can the authors improve the method to create reasoning trees of varying depths?
What are the implications of the results on Amazon Mechanical Turk for future validation efforts?
Can the authors provide more details on the minor editing of prompts and introduction of more detailed self-refine prompts required for weaker models?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge several limitations, including the potential for shallower trees to provide the best level of detail for creating a narrative, the challenges of using weaker models to create examples approaching the quality of GPT-4, and the need for more detailed self-refine prompts.

The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the method in reducing hallucinations in LVLMs.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Soft Mixture Denoising (SMD), a tractable relaxation of a Gaussian mixture model for modeling the denoising posterior in diffusion models. The authors prove that SMD permits diffusion models to well approximate any Gaussian mixture distributions and show that SMD significantly improves the generation quality of different diffusion models.
strengths: The paper provides a clear and concise introduction to the problem of expressive bottleneck in diffusion models and proposes a novel solution, Soft Mixture Denoising (SMD).
The authors provide a thorough theoretical analysis of the limitations of current diffusion models and prove that SMD can overcome these limitations.
The paper presents experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of SMD in improving the generation quality of different diffusion models.
The authors provide a clear and well-organized presentation of the paper, making it easy to follow and understand.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the data distribution is a Gaussian mixture, which may not be the case in all scenarios.
The authors do not provide a clear explanation of how to choose the number of mixture components in SMD.
The paper does not provide a thorough comparison with other methods that address the expressive bottleneck in diffusion models.
Some of the experimental results are not thoroughly explained, and it is not clear how the results were obtained.
questions: How does the choice of the number of mixture components in SMD affect the performance of the model?
Can SMD be extended to handle non-Gaussian data distributions?
How does SMD compare with other methods that address the expressive bottleneck in diffusion models?
Can the authors provide more details on how the experimental results were obtained?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge that the paper assumes a Gaussian mixture data distribution, which may not be the case in all scenarios. They also note that the choice of the number of mixture components in SMD is not clear and may require further investigation.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel training mechanism called FMN (Forget-Me-Not) to strengthen the durability of backdoor attacks against finetuning-based defenses.
strengths: The paper presents a comprehensive review of existing backdoor attacks and defenses, providing a clear understanding of the problem.
The proposed FMN training mechanism is well-designed and effectively strengthens the backdoor's durability against finetuning-based defenses.
The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of FMN in maintaining high attack success rates against various finetuning-based defenses.
The paper also conducts ablation studies to validate the importance of the cyclical learning rate and clean-backdoor interleaved training in FMN.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the attackers have full access to the backdoor model training, which might not always be true in practice.
The experimental results are limited to three benchmark datasets, and it would be beneficial to evaluate FMN on a broader range of datasets.
The paper does not provide a thorough analysis of the potential risks and limitations of FMN, which could be an important consideration for users and model providers.
questions: How does FMN perform against other types of defenses, such as data and test-time defenses?
Can FMN be adapted to craft an attack that can survive finetuning in black-box settings?
What are the potential risks and limitations of using FMN in practice, and how can they be mitigated?
reviews.limitations: The paper acknowledges that FMN requires the attackers to have full access to the backdoor model training, which might not always be true in practice.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel preconditioner, called CASPR, for deep neural network optimization that combines axes preconditioners through Kronecker approximation. The authors show that CASPR is ordered between Shampoo and full-matrix Adagrad in Loewner order, and demonstrate better approximation error and convergence guarantees compared to Shampoo and other adaptive regularization methods.
strengths: The paper proposes a novel and interesting approach to preconditioning in deep neural network optimization.
The authors provide a thorough analysis of the proposed method, including convergence guarantees and comparison with existing methods.
The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of CASPR in various deep learning tasks.
The paper is well-written and easy to follow, with clear explanations and concise notation.
weaknesses: The paper assumes a specific structure for the gradient matrix, which may not be applicable to all deep learning models.
The authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed method.
The experimental results are limited to a few specific tasks and datasets, and it would be interesting to see more comprehensive evaluations.
The paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the potential negative societal impact of the proposed method.
questions: How does the proposed method perform on more complex deep learning models, such as those with non-linear activation functions or batch normalization?
Can the authors provide more detailed analysis of the computational complexity of the proposed method?
How does the proposed method compare to other preconditioning methods, such as those based on the Fisher information matrix?
What are the potential negative societal impacts of the proposed method, and how can they be mitigated?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge that the proposed method assumes a specific structure for the gradient matrix, which may not be applicable to all deep learning models.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Split-N-Denoise (SnD), a framework that integrates split inference and denoising techniques to protect LLM inference with local differential privacy. The authors demonstrate that SnD outperforms existing DP-based baselines by over 10% on average, and maintains utility even in extremely low privacy budget settings.
strengths: The authors provide a comprehensive review of existing privacy-preserving LLM inference approaches and highlight the limitations of current methods.
The proposed SnD framework is well-designed and effectively addresses the challenges of local differential privacy in LLM inference.
The authors conduct extensive experiments to evaluate the performance of SnD on various LLM architectures and downstream tasks, and provide a thorough analysis of the results.
The paper includes a detailed discussion of the denoise model and its training procedure, which is a key component of the SnD framework.
weaknesses: The authors do not provide a clear explanation of the denoise model's architecture and its hyperparameters, which makes it difficult to understand the model's behavior.
The paper assumes that the user has access to the raw token representations and specific noise levels, which may not be feasible in practice.
The authors do not provide a thorough analysis of the computational overhead of the SnD framework, which is an important consideration for practical implementation.
The paper does not discuss the potential risks and limitations of the SnD framework, such as the potential for inference attacks or the impact on model accuracy.
questions: How does the denoise model's architecture and hyperparameters affect its performance?
What are the potential risks and limitations of the SnD framework, and how can they be mitigated?
How does the SnD framework perform on larger language models, and what are the implications for practical implementation?
reviews.limitations: The authors acknowledge that the SnD framework has limitations, including the assumption that the user has access to raw token representations and specific noise levels, and the potential for inference attacks. However, they do not provide a thorough analysis of these limitations and their implications.

reviews.summary: LipVoicer is a novel method for generating high-quality speech from silent videos by incorporating the text modality, leveraging a lip-reading network to predict the spoken text and a diffusion model to generate mel-spectrograms.
strengths: The paper presents a well-structured and clear approach to lip-to-speech synthesis, leveraging the text modality to improve the quality and intelligibility of the generated speech.
The authors provide a thorough evaluation of their method on challenging datasets, including LRS2 and LRS3, and demonstrate its superiority over existing baselines.
The paper includes a comprehensive ablation study, examining the influence of hyperparameters and modules on the performance of LipVoicer.
The authors provide a clear and concise explanation of their method, including the use of classifier-free guidance and the importance of the ASR guidance mechanism.
weaknesses: The paper could benefit from a more detailed explanation of the lip-reading network and its training procedure.
The authors do not provide a thorough comparison of their method with other state-of-the-art lip-to-speech synthesis techniques.
The paper could benefit from a more comprehensive evaluation of the robustness of LipVoicer to different types of noise and distortions.
The authors do not provide a clear explanation of the hyperparameter tuning procedure used to optimize the performance of LipVoicer.
questions: How does the lip-reading network handle cases where the spoken text is ambiguous or difficult to predict?
Can the authors provide more details on the training procedure for the lip-reading network and the diffusion model?
How does the ASR guidance mechanism handle cases where the predicted text does not significantly overlap with the ground truth text?
Can the authors provide more information on the hyperparameter tuning procedure used to optimize the performance of LipVoicer?

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel metric learning framework, PEEP, to predict enzyme functionality, which integrates biology priors of protein functionality to regularize the model learning.
strengths: The paper presents a comprehensive overview of the related work in enzyme function prediction and highlights the limitations of existing approaches.

The proposed PEEP framework incorporates biology-aware designs to regularize the learning of protein functionality, including integrating cognate ligands' embeddings, attentive learning of active sites, and considering functionality label hierarchy.
The paper presents extensive experimental results on multiple public benchmarks, demonstrating the superiority of PEEP over existing methods.
The authors conduct ablation studies to assess the efficacy of integrating biological priors within the framework, which provides valuable insights into the design choices.
The paper explores different approaches to extract and fuse the representations of ligands, which is a novel contribution to the field of protein function prediction.
weaknesses: The paper assumes that the cognate ligands' embeddings are available, which may not be the case for all enzymes.
The authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the computational cost of the proposed framework.
The paper does not discuss the potential limitations of using a single pre-trained feature extractor for all proteins.
The authors do not provide a clear explanation of how the EC-aware regularization loss is computed.
The paper does not discuss the potential applications of the proposed framework beyond enzyme function prediction.

What are the potential applications of the proposed framework beyond enzyme function prediction?
